threat.title: Kubernetes Privilege Escalation via RBAC Misconfiguration
threat.description: Overly permissive Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) configurations in Kubernetes clusters allow attackers with limited pod access to escalate privileges to cluster administrator. This occurs when service accounts are granted excessive permissions such as pod creation, pod/exec access, or ability to bind privileged roles. Attackers can exploit these permissions to deploy privileged containers, execute commands in sensitive pods, or create new admin-level service accounts.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker compromises a low-privilege pod in the cluster through application vulnerability (e.g., RCE in web service)
2. Discovers the pod's service account has overly permissive RBAC role allowing pod creation or pod/exec access
3. Creates a new privileged pod with hostPath volume mounts to access node filesystem
4. Uses privileged pod to escape to the underlying node and access the host's /var/lib/kubelet/kubeconfig
5. Extracts cluster-admin credentials or creates new ClusterRoleBinding granting admin access to compromised service account
6. Gains full cluster control: can read secrets, deploy backdoors, pivot to other pods, or exfiltrate sensitive data across all namespaces
prerequisites: Compromised pod or service account with cluster access
Overly permissive ClusterRole or Role granting pod creation, pod/exec, or role binding permissions
Lack of Pod Security Standards/Policies enforcement
No admission controller validation of privileged pod specifications

cve_references.description: Kubernetes privilege escalation via crafted API requests allowing unauthorized access to backend services. Attackers could establish a connection through the Kubernetes API server and send arbitrary requests over the same connection directly to backend services.

Kubernetes API server allows users with limited namespace permissions to access cluster-scoped resources. Attackers could craft malicious requests to read or modify cluster-wide custom resources despite having only namespace-level access.

Kubernetes API server denial of service vulnerability allowing unauthenticated users to perform resource exhaustion through YAML parsing bombs, often exploited in combination with privilege escalation attacks.

real_world_breaches.impact: Attackers gained access to Tesla's Kubernetes console, which was not password protected, and deployed cryptomining malware. AWS credentials were exposed in pod environment variables, leading to S3 bucket compromise containing proprietary data.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Exposed Kubernetes dashboard with no authentication, overly permissive service account permissions allowing pod deployment, and secrets stored as environment variables

real_world_breaches.incident: Shopify Kubernetes Cluster Privilege Escalation (Disclosed via Bug Bounty)
real_world_breaches.impact: Security researcher demonstrated ability to escalate from compromised pod to cluster-admin access through overly permissive RBAC configuration. Awarded $25,000 bounty.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Service accounts in production namespaces had excessive permissions including pod/exec and role binding capabilities

real_world_breaches.incident: Microsoft Azure Container Instances Privilege Escalation (Azurescape)
real_world_breaches.impact: Cross-tenant container escape vulnerability in Azure Container Instances allowed attackers to execute code on underlying Kubernetes nodes and potentially access other customers' containers.
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Insufficient container isolation and overly permissive pod security contexts in multi-tenant Kubernetes environment

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Kubernetes RBAC misconfiguration allowing privilege escalation from namespace user to cluster admin
Pod creation permissions combined with privileged security context allowing node compromise

security_research.title: Kubernetes RBAC Security: Common Pitfalls and Best Practices
security_research.key_finding: 90% of surveyed Kubernetes clusters had at least one overly permissive RBAC configuration. Most common issue: service accounts with pod creation or pod/exec permissions without justification.

security_research.title: Attacking and Defending Kubernetes: Deep Dive into RBAC Escalation
security_research.key_finding: Demonstrated 17 different privilege escalation paths through RBAC misconfigurations, including pod creation, node proxy access, and custom resource manipulation

mitigations.title: Implement Least Privilege RBAC with Namespace Isolation
mitigations.description: Apply principle of least privilege by creating minimal RBAC roles with only necessary permissions. Avoid granting cluster-wide permissions (ClusterRole/ClusterRoleBinding) unless absolutely required. Use namespace-specific Roles and RoleBindings to limit blast radius. Never grant wildcard permissions (*) on critical resources like pods, secrets, or role bindings.

code_example.code:
---
# INSECURE EXAMPLE - DO NOT USE
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: insecure-pod-admin
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods", "pods/exec"]
  verbs: ["*"]  # ❌ DANGEROUS: Wildcard permissions
- apiGroups: ["rbac.authorization.k8s.io"]
  resources: ["*"]
  verbs: ["*"]  # ❌ CRITICAL: Can modify RBAC policies

---
# SECURE EXAMPLE - Least Privilege Role
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role  # Namespace-scoped, not ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: secure-app-role
  namespace: production
rules:
# Only allow reading pods in this namespace
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]  # ✅ Read-only
  resourceNames: []  # Can further restrict to specific pod names

# Allow reading ConfigMaps for application configuration
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["configmaps"]
  verbs: ["get"]
  resourceNames: ["app-config"]  # ✅ Specific ConfigMap only

# Allow reading Secrets (if absolutely necessary, prefer mounted secrets)
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["secrets"]
  verbs: ["get"]
  resourceNames: ["app-database-credentials"]  # ✅ Named secret only

# NO pod creation, NO pod/exec, NO role binding permissions ✅

---
# Bind role to specific service account
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: secure-app-binding
  namespace: production
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: app-service-account
  namespace: production
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: secure-app-role
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

---
# Service Account with automountServiceAccountToken disabled by default
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: app-service-account
  namespace: production
automountServiceAccountToken: false  # ✅ Prevent automatic token mounting

---
# Pod specification with explicit service account
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-app-pod
  namespace: production
spec:
  serviceAccountName: app-service-account
  automountServiceAccountToken: true  # Only mount when needed
  
  # Additional security hardening
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 10001
    fsGroup: 10001
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:1.0.0
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]  # Drop all Linux capabilities
    resources:
      limits:
        memory: "512Mi"
        cpu: "500m"
      requests:
        memory: "256Mi"
        cpu: "250m"

iso27001_controls: A.9.2.3 - Management of privileged access rights
nist_csf: PR.PT-3 - Least functionality principle is incorporated
validation_steps: Audit all ClusterRoles and Roles for wildcard permissions: kubectl get clusterroles -o yaml | grep -E "verbs:.*\*|resources:.*\*"
List all service accounts with pod/exec permissions: kubectl-who-can exec pods --all-namespaces
Review ClusterRoleBindings for over-privileged service accounts: kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o wide
Test service account permissions: kubectl auth can-i --list --as=system:serviceaccount:production:app-service-account
Enable RBAC audit logging in kube-apiserver with --audit-policy-file

mitigations.title: Enforce Pod Security Standards with Admission Controllers
mitigations.description: Replace deprecated PodSecurityPolicy with Pod Security Standards (PSS) and Pod Security Admission controller (available since Kubernetes 1.23). Enforce 'restricted' profile in production namespaces to prevent privileged pods, host namespace access, and dangerous volume mounts. Use Open Policy Agent (OPA) Gatekeeper or Kyverno for advanced policy enforcement.

code_example.code:
---
# Namespace with Pod Security Standards enforcement
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: production
  labels:
    # Enforce 'restricted' Pod Security Standard
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce-version: v1.28
    
    # Audit violations without blocking (for testing)
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit: restricted
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit-version: v1.28
    
    # Warn users about violations
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn: restricted
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn-version: v1.28

---
# Alternative: Cluster-wide Pod Security Configuration
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: pod-security-config
  namespace: kube-system
data:
  # Applied to kube-apiserver via --admission-control-config-file
  pod-security-config.yaml: |
    apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
    kind: AdmissionConfiguration
    plugins:
    - name: PodSecurity
      configuration:
        apiVersion: pod-security.admission.config.k8s.io/v1
        kind: PodSecurityConfiguration
        defaults:
          enforce: "restricted"
          enforce-version: "v1.28"
          audit: "restricted"
          audit-version: "v1.28"
          warn: "restricted"
          warn-version: "v1.28"
        exemptions:
          usernames: []  # No user exemptions
          runtimeClasses: []  # No runtime class exemptions
          namespaces:
          - kube-system  # Only exempt kube-system namespace
          - ingress-nginx  # Ingress controllers may need privileged access

---
# OPA Gatekeeper: Prevent privileged pods
apiVersion: templates.gatekeeper.sh/v1
kind: ConstraintTemplate
metadata:
  name: k8spspprivilegedcontainer
  annotations:
    description: Prevents creation of privileged containers
spec:
  crd:
    spec:
      names:
        kind: K8sPSPPrivilegedContainer
  targets:
  - target: admission.k8s.gatekeeper.sh
    rego: |
      package k8spspprivilegedcontainer
      
      violation[{"msg": msg}] {
        container := input.review.object.spec.containers[_]
        container.securityContext.privileged
        msg := sprintf("Privileged container not allowed: %v", [container.name])
      }
      
      violation[{"msg": msg}] {
        container := input.review.object.spec.initContainers[_]
        container.securityContext.privileged
        msg := sprintf("Privileged init container not allowed: %v", [container.name])
      }

---
# Apply the constraint to production namespace
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: K8sPSPPrivilegedContainer
metadata:
  name: deny-privileged-containers
spec:
  match:
    kinds:
    - apiGroups: [""]
      kinds: ["Pod"]
    namespaces:
    - production
    - staging
  parameters: {}

---
# Kyverno Policy: Block dangerous volume mounts
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: restrict-volume-mounts
  annotations:
    policies.kyverno.io/title: Restrict Volume Mounts
    policies.kyverno.io/severity: high
    policies.kyverno.io/description: >-
      Prevents pods from mounting dangerous host paths like /var/run/docker.sock,
      /var/lib/kubelet, and root filesystem.
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce  # Block non-compliant pods
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: check-host-path-volumes
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: >-
        HostPath volumes are not allowed except for approved paths.
        Blocked paths include: /var/run/docker.sock, /var/lib/kubelet, /, /etc, /proc
      deny:
        conditions:
          any:
          - key: "{{ request.object.spec.volumes[?hostPath.path].hostPath.path }}"
            operator: AnyIn
            value:
            - "/var/run/docker.sock"
            - "/var/lib/kubelet"
            - "/var/lib/kubelet/*"
            - "/"
            - "/etc"
            - "/etc/*"
            - "/proc"
            - "/proc/*"

---
# Kyverno Policy: Require runAsNonRoot
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-run-as-nonroot
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: check-runAsNonRoot
    match:
      any:
      - resources:
          kinds:
          - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Containers must run as non-root user"
      pattern:
        spec:
          securityContext:
            runAsNonRoot: true
          containers:
          - securityContext:
              allowPrivilegeEscalation: false

nist_csf: PR.IP-1 - Configuration management baseline is created
validation_steps: Enable Pod Security Admission controller in kube-apiserver: --enable-admission-plugins=PodSecurity
Test privileged pod creation (should be blocked): kubectl run test --image=nginx --privileged -n production
Verify constraints are enforced: kubectl get constraints
Audit existing pods for violations: kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json | kubectl-check-pss

mitigations.title: Deploy Validating Admission Webhooks for RBAC and Security Context Validation
mitigations.description: Implement custom validating admission webhooks to enforce organization-specific security policies beyond built-in Pod Security Standards. Validate RBAC role bindings before creation, reject service accounts with excessive permissions, and enforce mandatory security contexts. Use Falco for runtime threat detection of privilege escalation attempts.

code_example.code:
---
# Validating Admission Webhook Configuration
apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: ValidatingWebhookConfiguration
metadata:
  name: security-validation-webhook
webhooks:
- name: rbac-validator.security.company.com
  clientConfig:
    service:
      name: security-webhook-service
      namespace: security-system
      path: "/validate-rbac"
    caBundle: LS0tLS1CRUdJTi... # Base64 encoded CA cert
  rules:
  - operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
    apiGroups: ["rbac.authorization.k8s.io"]
    apiVersions: ["v1"]
    resources: ["clusterrolebindings", "rolebindings"]
  - operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
    apiGroups: [""]
    apiVersions: ["v1"]
    resources: ["serviceaccounts"]
  admissionReviewVersions: ["v1"]
  sideEffects: None
  failurePolicy: Fail  # Block on webhook failure (fail-closed)
  timeoutSeconds: 10
  namespaceSelector:
    matchExpressions:
    - key: security-webhook
      operator: NotIn
      values: ["ignore"]

- name: pod-security-validator.security.company.com
  clientConfig:
    service:
      name: security-webhook-service
      namespace: security-system
      path: "/validate-pod"
    caBundle: LS0tLS1CRUdJTi...
  rules:
  - operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
    apiGroups: [""]
    apiVersions: ["v1"]
    resources: ["pods"]
  admissionReviewVersions: ["v1"]
  sideEffects: None
  failurePolicy: Fail
  timeoutSeconds: 5

---
# Example Webhook Service Implementation (Go)
# File: cmd/webhook/main.go
# Build with: docker build -t security-webhook:1.0 .
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: webhook-go-code
  namespace: security-system
data:
  main.go: |
    package main
    
    import (
        "encoding/json"
        "fmt"
        "net/http"
        
        admissionv1 "k8s.io/api/admission/v1"
        rbacv1 "k8s.io/api/rbac/v1"
        metav1 "k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/apis/meta/v1"
    )
    
    // Dangerous RBAC verbs that require explicit approval
    var dangerousVerbs = []string{"*", "create", "delete", "escalate", "bind", "impersonate"}
    var dangerousResources = []string{"*", "pods/exec", "pods/attach", "secrets", "clusterroles", "clusterrolebindings"}
    
    func validateRBAC(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) {
        var admissionReview admissionv1.AdmissionReview
        json.NewDecoder(r.Body).Decode(&admissionReview)
        
        response := &admissionv1.AdmissionResponse{
            UID:     admissionReview.Request.UID,
            Allowed: true,
        }
        
        // Parse RoleBinding or ClusterRoleBinding
        if admissionReview.Request.Kind.Kind == "ClusterRoleBinding" {
            var binding rbacv1.ClusterRoleBinding
            json.Unmarshal(admissionReview.Request.Object.Raw, &binding)
            
            // Block binding to cluster-admin unless explicitly annotated
            if binding.RoleRef.Name == "cluster-admin" {
                if _, ok := binding.Annotations["security.company.com/approved"]; !ok {
                    response.Allowed = false
                    response.Result = &metav1.Status{
                        Message: "ClusterRoleBinding to cluster-admin requires security approval annotation",
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        
        admissionReview.Response = response
        json.NewEncoder(w).Encode(admissionReview)
    }
    
    func main() {
        http.HandleFunc("/validate-rbac", validateRBAC)
        http.ListenAndServeTLS(":8443", "/certs/tls.crt", "/certs/tls.key", nil)
    }

---
# Deploy webhook service
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: security-webhook
  namespace: security-system
spec:
  replicas: 2  # High availability
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: security-webhook
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: security-webhook
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: security-webhook-sa
      containers:
      - name: webhook
        image: company/security-webhook:1.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8443
          name: webhook
          protocol: TCP
        volumeMounts:
        - name: webhook-certs
          mountPath: /certs
          readOnly: true
        resources:
          limits:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
          requests:
            memory: "128Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
        livenessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8443
            scheme: HTTPS
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 10
      volumes:
      - name: webhook-certs
        secret:
          secretName: security-webhook-certs

---
# Falco Runtime Security Rules for Privilege Escalation Detection
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: falco-rules-custom
  namespace: falco-system
data:
  custom-rules.yaml: |
    - rule: Kubernetes Privilege Escalation Detected
      desc: Detects attempts to escalate privileges via pod creation or RBAC modification
      condition: >
        (k8s.verb in (create, update, patch) and
         (k8s.target.resource in (pods, clusterrolebindings, rolebindings)) and
         k8s.req.pod.privileged=true)
        or
        (k8s.verb in (create, update) and
         k8s.target.resource = clusterrolebindings and
         k8s.target.name = cluster-admin)
      output: >
        Kubernetes privilege escalation attempt detected
        (user=%k8s.user.name namespace=%k8s.ns.name resource=%k8s.target.resource
         verb=%k8s.verb privileged=%k8s.req.pod.privileged)
      priority: CRITICAL
      source: k8s_audit
      tags: [k8s, rbac, privilege_escalation]
    
    - rule: Pod Created with Dangerous Capabilities
      desc: Detects pod creation with CAP_SYS_ADMIN or other dangerous capabilities
      condition: >
        k8s.verb = create and
        k8s.target.resource = pods and
        (k8s.req.pod.capabilities.add intersects (CAP_SYS_ADMIN, CAP_NET_ADMIN, CAP_SYS_PTRACE))
      output: >
        Pod created with dangerous capabilities
        (user=%k8s.user.name pod=%k8s.target.name namespace=%k8s.ns.name
         capabilities=%k8s.req.pod.capabilities.add)
      priority: WARNING
      source: k8s_audit
      tags: [k8s, capabilities]
    
    - rule: Service Account Token Access from Suspicious Process
      desc: Detects processes reading service account tokens from unusual locations
      condition: >
        open_read and
        fd.name startswith /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount and
        not proc.name in (kubelet, kube-proxy, allowed-app)
      output: >
        Suspicious service account token access
        (proc=%proc.name pid=%proc.pid file=%fd.name user=%user.name container=%container.name)
      priority: WARNING
      source: syscall
      tags: [k8s, service_account, suspicious]

---
# Deploy Falco DaemonSet
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: falco
  namespace: falco-system
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: falco
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: falco
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: falco
      hostNetwork: true
      hostPID: true
      containers:
      - name: falco
        image: falcosecurity/falco:0.36.2
        args:
        - /usr/bin/falco
        - -K
        - /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token
        - -k
        - https://kubernetes.default
        - -pk
        securityContext:
          privileged: true  # Required for kernel module access
        volumeMounts:
        - name: dev
          mountPath: /host/dev
        - name: proc
          mountPath: /host/proc
          readOnly: true
        - name: boot
          mountPath: /host/boot
          readOnly: true
        - name: lib-modules
          mountPath: /host/lib/modules
          readOnly: true
        - name: usr
          mountPath: /host/usr
          readOnly: true
        - name: falco-rules
          mountPath: /etc/falco/rules.d
      volumes:
      - name: dev
        hostPath:
          path: /dev
      - name: proc
        hostPath:
          path: /proc
      - name: boot
        hostPath:
          path: /boot
      - name: lib-modules
        hostPath:
          path: /lib/modules
      - name: usr
        hostPath:
          path: /usr
      - name: falco-rules
        configMap:
          name: falco-rules-custom

tools.name: Falco
tools.cost: Free (Open Source) / Enterprise features via Sysdig

nist_csf: DE.AE-3 - Event data is aggregated and correlated
validation_steps: Generate webhook certificates: kubectl cert-manager or manual openssl
Deploy webhook service and register ValidatingWebhookConfiguration
Test RBAC validation: kubectl create clusterrolebinding test-admin --clusterrole=cluster-admin --serviceaccount=default:test (should be blocked)
Install Falco: helm install falco falcosecurity/falco --namespace falco-system
Test Falco detection: kubectl run privileged-pod --image=nginx --privileged (should generate Falco alert)
Configure Falco alerts to SIEM: Edit falco.yaml to send alerts to Azure Sentinel, Splunk, or Elasticsearch

indicators: Service accounts with cluster-admin or edit ClusterRoleBinding that weren't explicitly approved
Pod creation events from service accounts in unexpected namespaces
Pod specifications with privileged: true or hostPath volumes mounting sensitive paths
Frequent pod/exec or pod/attach API calls from service accounts
ClusterRoleBinding or RoleBinding modifications outside of approved change windows
Authentication attempts using service account tokens from external IP addresses
Pod creation with capabilities like CAP_SYS_ADMIN or CAP_NET_ADMIN

log_queries.query:
// Detect privilege escalation via pod creation with privileged security context
let SuspiciousNamespaces = dynamic(["production", "staging", "finance"]);
KubePodInventory
| where TimeGenerated > ago(24h)
| where Namespace in (SuspiciousNamespaces)
| where PodStatus == "Running"
| extend SecurityContext = parse_json(tostring(PodSecurityContext))
| extend IsPrivileged = SecurityContext.privileged == true
| extend HostNetwork = SecurityContext.hostNetwork == true
| extend HostPID = SecurityContext.hostPID == true
| where IsPrivileged or HostNetwork or HostPID
| extend Capabilities = parse_json(tostring(SecurityContext.capabilities.add))
| where array_length(Capabilities) > 0
| project TimeGenerated, Namespace, PodName, ServiceAccount, IsPrivileged, HostNetwork, HostPID, Capabilities, Node
| join kind=inner (
    AzureDiagnostics
    | where Category == "kube-audit"
    | where verb_s == "create"
    | where objectRef_resource_s == "pods"
    | project TimeGenerated, user_username_s, sourceIPs_s, userAgent_s, objectRef_namespace_s, objectRef_name_s
) on $left.PodName == $right.objectRef_name_s and $left.Namespace == $right.objectRef_namespace_s
| project TimeGenerated, Namespace, PodName, CreatedBy=user_username_s, SourceIP=sourceIPs_s, UserAgent=userAgent_s, IsPrivileged, HostNetwork, HostPID, Capabilities

// Detect unauthorized ClusterRoleBinding to cluster-admin
AzureDiagnostics
| where Category == "kube-audit"
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1h)
| where verb_s in ("create", "update", "patch")
| where objectRef_resource_s in ("clusterrolebindings", "rolebindings")
| extend RoleRef = parse_json(tostring(requestObject_s)).roleRef.name
| where RoleRef in ("cluster-admin", "admin", "edit")
| extend Subject = parse_json(tostring(requestObject_s)).subjects
| extend ServiceAccountName = tostring(Subject[0].name)
| extend ServiceAccountNamespace = tostring(Subject[0].namespace)
| where user_username_s !startswith "system:serviceaccount:kube-system"
| where user_username_s !contains "admin@company.com"  // Whitelist approved admins
| project TimeGenerated, User=user_username_s, SourceIP=sourceIPs_s, Verb=verb_s, Resource=objectRef_resource_s, 
          ResourceName=objectRef_name_s, RoleRef, ServiceAccountName, ServiceAccountNamespace, UserAgent=userAgent_s
| extend Severity = iff(RoleRef == "cluster-admin", "Critical", "High")

// Privilege escalation via pod/exec to access node resources
sequence by user.name with maxspan=5m
  [any where event.dataset == "kubernetes.audit" and 
   kubernetes.audit.verb == "create" and 
   kubernetes.audit.objectRef.resource == "pods" and
   kubernetes.audit.requestObject.spec.containers[0].securityContext.privileged == true]
  [any where event.dataset == "kubernetes.audit" and
   kubernetes.audit.verb == "create" and
   kubernetes.audit.objectRef.subresource == "exec" and
   kubernetes.audit.requestObject.command contains "chroot" or
   kubernetes.audit.requestObject.command contains "nsenter"]

index=kubernetes sourcetype=kube:apiserver:audit
| spath input=_raw
| search verb IN ("create", "update", "patch")
| search objectRef.resource IN ("pods", "clusterrolebindings", "rolebindings")
| eval is_privileged = if(like(requestObject, "%privileged%true%"), 1, 0)
| eval role_escalation = if(like(requestObject, "%roleRef%cluster-admin%"), 1, 0)
| where is_privileged=1 OR role_escalation=1
| eval user_type = case(
    like(user.username, "system:serviceaccount:%"), "ServiceAccount",
    like(user.username, "system:%"), "SystemUser",
    1=1, "HumanUser"
  )
| table _time, user.username, user_type, sourceIPs{}, verb, objectRef.resource, objectRef.name, objectRef.namespace, is_privileged, role_escalation
| sort - _time

# Query Falco alerts from syslog or Elasticsearch
index=falco-alerts priority=CRITICAL
| search rule_name="Kubernetes Privilege Escalation Detected" OR 
         rule_name="Pod Created with Dangerous Capabilities" OR
         rule_name="Service Account Token Access from Suspicious Process"
| stats count by rule_name, k8s.user.name, k8s.ns.name, k8s.target.resource, k8s.target.name
| where count > 1
